coredata.dc:title: Dual S-scheme MoS2/ZnIn2S4/Graphene quantum dots ternary heterojunctions for highly efficient photocatalytic hydrogen evolution
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Inc.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Inc.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Ning
Ma, Jiafeng
Wang, Wenlong
Chang, Qing
Liu, Lei
Hao, Caihong
Zhang, Huinian
Zhang, Huayang
Hu, Shengliang
Wang, Shaobin
coredata.dc:description: The layered chalcogenide ZnIn2S4 (ZIS) exhibits photo-stability and a tunable band gap but is limited in photocatalytic applications, such as hydrogen (H2) production, due to rapid carrier recombination and slow charge separation. To overcome these limitations, we have synthesized a ternary MoS2/ZIS/graphene quantum dots (GQDs) heterojunction, wherein MoS2 and GQDs are strategically attached to ZIS interlaced nanoflakes, enhancing light absorption across the 500–1500 nm range. This heterojunction benefits from dual S-scheme interfaces between MoS2-ZIS and ZIS-GQDs, establishing directed internal electric fields (IEFs). These IEFs accelerate the transfer of photoinduced electrons from the conduction bands of MoS2 and GQDs to the valence band of ZIS, promoting rapid recombination with holes and facilitating efficient catalytic reactions with plentiful photoinduced electrons stemmed from the conduction band of ZIS. As a result, the photocatalytic H2 production rate of the MoS2/ZIS/GQDs heterojunction is measured at 21.63 mmol h−1 g−1, marking an increase of 36.7 times over pure ZIS. This research provides valuable insights into designing novel heterojunctions for improved charge separation and transfer for solar energy conversion applications.
coredata.openaccessSponsorName: CAUL institutions 2023: Hybrid journals full funding
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ternary MoS2/ZIS/GQDs heterojunction
S-scheme
Photocatalytic hydrogen production
Charge separation and transfer
Interfacial electric field

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of MoS2 nanosheets and GQDs
Synthesis of the ternary MoS2/ZIS/GQDs heterojunction
The morphology, structure and composition characterizations

H. Zhang acknowledges the support from the Alexander von Humboldt Foundation (Humboldt Research Fellowship).

H. Zhang acknowledges the support from the Alexander von Humboldt Foundation (Humboldt Research Fellowship).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the preparation of MoS2/ZIS/GQDs heterojunction; (b) XRD patterns of different samples (Red frame: the expanded areas shown in supporting information); (c–d) SEM images of MoS2/ZIS/GQDs heterojunctions; (e–j) Element mapping images of In, Mo, Zn, S and C for MoS2/ZIS/GQDs sample measured by TEM-EDS; (k) AFM image with inset showing the height profile along the white line; (l) TEM and (m) HR-TEM of MoS2/ZIS/GQDs. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(a) In 3d, S 2p, Zn 2p and (b) C 1s, Mo 3d, Mo 3p XPS spectra for MoS2/ZIS/GQDs heterojunction, (c–d) In 3d, Zn 2p XPS spectra for different samples.

(a) H2 evolution rates of different samples; (b) H2 evolution amounts of different samples; (c) Recycling tests of photocatalytic H2 evolution of MoS210/ZIS/GQDs10 sample; (d) wavelength-dependent AQE at the wavelength of 400, 420, 500 and 600 nm over MoS210/ZIS/GQDs10 sample; (e) H2 evolution rates and AQE in comparison with previously reported ZIS-MoS2 and ZIS-CDs photocatalysts. (Note: the adding error bars in Fig. 3a, 3b and 3d were based on three repeat experiments).

(a) UV–Vis–NIR absorption spectra in the spectral region of 200–2500 nm; (b) Photocurrent response curves and (c) EIS plots of different samples, respectively; (d) Tauc plots of the GQDs, MoS2 and ZIS.
Mott-Schottky plots of the (a) MoS2, (b) ZIS and (c) GQDs, respectively; XPS valence spectrum of (d) MoS2, (e) ZIS and (f) GQDs sample heterostructures after calibration, respectively.

UPS spectra of the (a) MoS2, (b) ZIS and (c) GQDs, respectively, (d) Band structures of MoS2, ZIS and GQDs, (e) Mechanism of photocatalytic H2 evolution over MoS2/ZIS/GQDs heterojunction, (f) ESR spectra of DMPO-⋅O2 − and DMPO-⋅OH tested after irradiating for 0 min over different photocatalysts, (g) ESR spectra of DMPO-⋅O2 −after irradiating for 10 min, (h) ESR spectra of DMPO-⋅OH after irradiating for 10 min.
Comparison of the recently reported ZIS-based photocatalysts for H2 evolution.

head.ce:title: Dual S-scheme MoS2/ZnIn2S4/Graphene quantum dots ternary heterojunctions for highly efficient photocatalytic hydrogen evolution

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Energy and Power Engineering & State Key Laboratory of Coal and CBM Co-Mining, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chair for Photonics and Optoelectronics, Nano-Institute Munich, Department of Physics, Ludwig-Maximilians-University Munich, 80539 Munich, Germany

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, The University of Adelaide, North Terrace Campus, Adelaide, SA 5005, Australia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, The University of Adelaide, North Terrace Campus, Adelaide, SA 5005, Australia

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Chair for Photonics and Optoelectronics, Nano-Institute Munich, Department of Physics, Ludwig-Maximilians-University Munich, 80539 Munich, Germany.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The layered chalcogenide ZnIn2S4 (ZIS) exhibits photo-stability and a tunable band gap but is limited in photocatalytic applications, such as hydrogen (H2) production, due to rapid carrier recombination and slow charge separation. To overcome these limitations, we have synthesized a ternary MoS2/ZIS/graphene quantum dots (GQDs) heterojunction, wherein MoS2 and GQDs are strategically attached to ZIS interlaced nanoflakes, enhancing light absorption across the 500–1500 nm range. This heterojunction benefits from dual S-scheme interfaces between MoS2-ZIS and ZIS-GQDs, establishing directed internal electric fields (IEFs). These IEFs accelerate the transfer of photoinduced electrons from the conduction bands of MoS2 and GQDs to the valence band of ZIS, promoting rapid recombination with holes and facilitating efficient catalytic reactions with plentiful photoinduced electrons stemmed from the conduction band of ZIS. As a result, the photocatalytic H2 production rate of the MoS2/ZIS/GQDs heterojunction is measured at 21.63 mmol h−1 g−1, marking an increase of 36.7 times over pure ZIS. This research provides valuable insights into designing novel heterojunctions for improved charge separation and transfer for solar energy conversion applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As a sustainable and clean energy, hydrogen (H2) is crucial for tackling future energy shortages and environmental challenges [1–3]. Among various H2 production strategies, photocatalytic water splitting using solar energy offers a green and promising solution [2–5]. The effectiveness of this method largely depends on the development of advanced photocatalysts. The layered chalcogenide ZnIn2S4 (ZIS) has emerged as a promising candidate due to its favorable proton adsorption features, great photo-stability, low toxicity, adjustable band gap, etc. [5–9]. For example, Xi et al. reported a photocatalytic H2 activity of 1.03 mmol h−1 g−1 using pristine ZIS [10]. However, challenges such as inefficient charge separation and migration, limited solar absorption, and slow reaction kinetics hinder ZIS's performance in photocatalytic applications [6,9,11].

Recently, various strategies such as element doping, defects/vacancy engineering, interface engineering and morphology modification have shown potential in tuning the band gap, enhancing the charge separation and increasing the exposure of active sites to enhance the photocatalytic performance [11,12]. Among these, constructing heterostructures by coupling ZIS with complementary semiconductors has shown promise. Different heterojunctions, including Schottky, type-Ⅰ and type-II junctions, have been designed to reduce recombination rate and enhance photocatalytic abilities [5,7–15]. For example, molybdenum disulfide (MoS2)/Cu-ZIS (type-Ⅰ) and MoS2/ZIS composites (type-II) were fabricated, which exhibits photocatalytic H2 activity of 5.463 mmol h−1 g−1 and 0.343 mmol h−1 g−1, respectively [16,17]. However, these heterojunctions often struggle with suboptimal band alignments that can lead to inefficient charge transfer and insufficient redox potential. Type-II heterojunction, characterized by staggered conduction band (CB) and valence band (VB) positions, allows photogenerated electrons to transfer from higher to lower CB energy levels and photoinduced holes to migrate from lower to high VB energy levels under irradiation, facing electrostatic repulsion issues that impede effective charge separation [18]. Moreover, the cumulative electrons in the lower CB energy level possess weak reduction potential, which is unfavorable for the photocatalytic reaction [19]. In response, S-scheme heterojunctions, introduced by Yu et al. in 2019 using a WO3/g-C3N4 system [20], offer a solution by combining reduction photocatalyst (RP) and oxidation photocatalyst (OP) with an internal electric field (IEF) at their interface to facilitate the photogenerated electrons of OP recombining with the photoinduced holes in the VB of the RP, maintaining high reduction potentials in the CB of RP [18,21,22]. To date, ZIS-based S-scheme heterojunctions like ZIS/CdS [23], ZIS/Cu2MoS4 [24], Co3O4@ZIS [25], WO3@ZIS [26], ZnWO4-ZIS [27], Sv-ZnS/ZIS [28], Cu2-xS@ZIS [29], CoAl-LDHs/ZIS [30], C/MoS2@ZIS/Co3O4 [31], etc. have been explored, yet they still present potential for further enhancement [32]. Improvements could be achieved by adopting a ternary heterojunction system with a dual S-scheme, which introduces an additional electron transport channel over binary systems, significantly enhancing light absorption, charge separation, and overall photocatalytic efficiency. This approach not only maximizes light utilization but also leverages dual interface-driven IEFs to more effectively separate and utilize photogenerated carriers [32].

Except ZIS, constructing a dual S-scheme heterojunction requires two other suitable semiconductor materials. Two-dimensional (2D) MoS2 nanosheets and graphene quantum dots (GQDs), both known for strong visible light absorption and long carrier lifetimes, respectively, possess adjustable band structures and short migration distances for photogenerated charge carriers, making them ideal components for constructing ZIS-based ternary heterojunctions [4,33–36]. Herein, 2D MoS2 nanosheets and GQDs were combined with ZIS to fabricate the ternary MoS2/ZIS/GQDs heterojunction using hydrothermal method. This configuration leverages the morphological benefits of MoS2 and GQDs—namely their 2D nanosheet structure and few-layer graphene thickness—to seamlessly integrate with the layered ZIS flower-like microspheres. The MoS2/ZIS/GQDs heterojunction exhibited improvement in the photocatalytic H2 evolution and apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) compared with the pristine ZIS, MoS2/ZIS and ZIS/GQDs heterojunctions, achieving an H2 evolution rate of 21. 63 mmol h−1 g−1, along with a high AQE of 17.60 % under 400 nm monochromatic light. Compared to pure ZIS, the enhanced light absorption capacity of the MoS2/ZIS/GQD heterojunction promoted the generation of photoinduced carriers. Comprehensive optical and electrochemical characterizations were performed to elucidate the mechanisms driving this enhancement. Enhanced light absorption, dual S-scheme architecture, presence of two internal electric fields (IEFs), and improved conductivity facilitated efficient carrier separation and conduction while maintaining robust redox capabilities, key factors contributing to the superior photocatalytic H2 evolution performance.

Zinc acetate dihydrate (Zn(CH3COO)2·2H2O, 99.99 % metal basis), Indium chloride (InCl3, 99.99 % metal basis), thioacetamide (CH3CSNH2, TAA, ≥98 %) and ascorbic acid (AA, >99 %) were purchased from Aladdin Biochem. Tech. Co., LTD. Molybdenum (IV) sulfide (MoS2, 99 % metals basis) was bought from Alfa Aesar Chemical Co., LTD. All chemical reagents were obtained from commercial sources of analytical reagent (AR) grade without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of MoS2 nanosheets and GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: MoS2 nanosheets were synthesized by using the liquid-phase exfoliation method. 150 mg of MoS2 powder was added into 100 mL of ethanol/water mixture (50 % EtOH by volume). After optimizing the sonication time, centrifugation speed and temperature, the solution was sonicated for 4 h at 15 °C and then centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 20 min. The obtained 100 mL supernatant contained approximately 15 mg of MoS2 (0.15 mg/mL). The carbon dots were fabricated on the basis of a previously reported method [37,38]. Typically, 200 mg coal pitch was mixed with formic acid and hydrogen peroxide (volume ratio 10:1). After sonicating for 22 h and centrifuging at 8000 rpm for 20 min, the carbon dots were isolated by evaporating the residual liquid and drying at 60 °C. Carbon dots were then added into 100 mL ethanol (50 %)/water (50 %) mixture. A 0.05 mg/mL GQDs suspension was further obtained via ultrasonic exfoliation (5 h, 15 °C) and centrifugation (10,000 rpm, 20 min) after optimizing these parameters.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the ternary MoS2/ZIS/GQDs heterojunction
ce:section.ce:para: Typically, InCl3 (1 mmol), Zn(CH3COO)2·2H2O (0.5 mmol) and TAA (4 mmol) were successively added in 10 mL (1.5 mg) of MoS2 supernatant (solution 1). Simultaneously, 10 mL (0.5 mg) of GQDs suspension was mixed into solution 1, then sonicated for 10 min and stirred for 30 min. Afterwards, the mixed solution was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the mixture was washed with ethanol and deionized water, centrifuged at 9000 r/min and dried at 60 °C for 10 h to obtain the final product (∼165 mg), named MoS210/ZIS/GQDs10 (10 mL MoS2 and 10 mL GQDs). Additionally, ZIS, MoS2/ZIS and ZIS/GQDs were synthesized under the same hydrothermal condition. Pure ZIS was prepared following the above steps, excluding MoS2 and GQDs. MoS2/ZIS samples were prepared without GQDs by adding 5, 15 and 20 mL of MoS2, and were named MoS25/ZIS, MoS215/ZIS, and MoS220/ZIS, respectively. Similarly, ZIS/GQDsx (x = 5, 10, 20 mL) samples were synthesized without adding MoS2.

The morphology and microstructure were measured by using a ZEISS Sigma 300 scanning electron microscope (SEM) equipped with an energy dispersive X-ray spectrometer (EDS), JEOL JEM-F200 transmission electron microscope (TEM) outfitted with the EDS and atomic force microscope (AFM). The crystalline and phase information were characterized by Rigaku Miniflex 600 X-ray diffraction (XRD). The composition and chemical states were investigated with Thermo ESCALAB 250 XI X X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and the XPS data were calibrated by the binding energy of C 1s spectrum (284.8 eV). Ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) analysis was implemented by using the Thermo Fisher ESCALAB 250Xi surface photoelectric effects to measure the cut-off energy values, with a photon emission energy of 21.22 eV through He I excitation. The light absorption properties were studied by using a Hitachi U4150 UV–vis spectrophotometer with barium sulfate as the standard reference. The electron spin resonance (ESR) measurements were conducted on Bruker EMX plus ESR spectrometer with a 9.054 GHz magnetic field to test the free radicals. The 5,5-dimethylpyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) and 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP) were adopted as spin-trapping reagents. The ⋅O2 −, ⋅OH, and 1O2 were tested in methanol and aqueous solution, respectively.

5 mg of photocatalysts were dispersed in 30 mL of an aqueous solution containing 0.1 M ascorbic acid by using sonication, and the mixture was placed in a closed quartz reactor. Ascorbic acid is a commonly used and effective hole-trapping agent, which has been used in the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution of Sv-ZnIn2S4/MoSe2 and rCdSe/SL-ZnIn2S4/MoSe2 heterojunctions [5,8]. Before the hydrogen evolution reaction, the reaction system was degassed for 30 min to remove the air in the reaction system thoroughly. The samples were then irradiated under the simulated solar light source (100 mW/cm2, AM 1.5G, 300–1100 nm) using a 300 W Xenon lamp for H2 generation (CEL-PF300-T8, beijing china education Au-Light technology Co., Ltd). The reaction temperature was maintained at ∼8 °C by circulating cooling water. The generated hydrogen was analyzed by GC 7900 gas chromatograph (N2 carrier gas).

The tests were conducted by a three-electrode system on Bio-logic SP 200 electrochemical workstation. Typically, 10 mg of samples was added into 100 μL ethanol before dispersion by ultrasound. The 100 μL solution was deposited on Indium tin oxide (ITO) conductive glass (2 cm × 3 cm) and then dried in a 60 °C vacuum for 1 h, preparing the working electrode. The dried ITO glass, platinum plate and Ag/AgCl (containing saturated potassium chloride electrolyte) were used as working electrodes, counter electrodes and reference electrodes, respectively, to constitute a three-electrode system. The electrolyte was 0.5 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution. The photocurrent response was tested under 0.4 V bias voltage and 300 W Xenon lamp, and the light source automatically turned on/off every 20 s. MS plots were collected from −0.4 to 0.4 V under 1, 2 and 3 kHz frequency and 0.005 V amplitude. EIS test was recorded in the frequency range from 1 KHz to 100 KHz with 0.025 V of the amplitude.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The morphology, structure and composition characterizations
ce:section.ce:para: The MoS2/ZIS/GQDs heterojunction was synthesized in-situ by a facile hydrothermal method, as displayed in Fig. 1 a. MoS2 and GQDs were uniformly dispersed and combined with ZIS. The crystal structure of the ZIS, ZIS/GQDs, MoS2/ZIS and MoS2/ZIS/GQDs were determined by XRD technique. The diffraction peaks at 21.6°, 27.7°, 30.4°, 39.8°,47.2°, 52.4° and 55.6° could be indexed to (006), (102), (104), (108), (110), (116) and (022) planes of the hexagonal ZIS (JCPDS: 65-2023) [5], respectively (Fig. 1b, Fig. S1a–b). No significant peaks were observed in the XRD patterns of these binary and ternary heterojunctions due to the low content of GQDs and MoS2 (shown in Sections 2.2 and 2.3). This phenomenon was also found in MoS2/ZIS and carbon dots (CDs)/ZIS photocatalysts [31,39]. A small phase peak at 14.4° corresponding to the (002) plane of hexagonal MoS2 (JCPDS: 65-7025) was observed when the MoS2 content was further increased (Fig. 1b, Fig. S1a), whereas no obvious peaks belonging to GQDs were detected (Fig. S1c). Subsequently, SEM was conducted to analyze the morphologies and microstructure of the as-synthesized ZIS and heterojunctions. As depicted in Fig. S2a–b, the ZIS exhibited flower-like microspheres with plentiful interconneted nanoflakes. After integration with MoS2 and GQDs, the surface of the ZIS microsphere was partially covered by MoS2 layers and GQDs (Fig. S2c, Fig. 1c–d).

Furthermore, element mapping images of MoS2/ZIS and MoS2/ZIS/GQDs heterojunctions were shown in Fig. S3 and Fig. 1e–j. Element mapping confirmed the uniform distribution of Mo, Zn, In, S in MoS2/ZIS and an addition of C in MoS2/ZIS/GQDs, indicating successful dispersal of MoS2 and GQDs on ZIS microspheres. TEM was used to determine the microstructures of the GQDs, MoS2, and MoS2/ZIS/GQDs heterojunction further. As illustrated in Fig. S4, the sonicated GQDs displayed uniform dispersion, with graphene's (101) facet characterized by a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm [40]. AFM image indicated that the thicknesses of the CDs were approximately 3.22 nm (Fig. S5) The GQDs were approximately double-layered with an average thickness of 1.35 nm (Fig. 1k) [35,41]. MoS2 showed distinct lattice fringes with d-spacings of 0.27 nm and 0.61 nm, corresponding to the (100) and (002) lattice planes of 2H-MoS2 [42,43], confirming its attachment to ZIS nanoflakes (Fig. S6a–c). Notably, Fig. 1l and 1m displayed the different lattice fringes with d-values of 0.21 nm, 0.27 nm, 0.32 nm and 0.41 nm, which is assigned to the (101) plane of GQDs, (100) plane of MoS2, (102) plane and (006) plane of ZIS [7,8], respectively. Fourier Transform Infrared spectra of GQDs and MoS2/ZIS/GQDs samples were shown in Fig. S7. The peaks of GQDs at ∼1142, ∼1401 and ∼1605 cm−1 corresponded to COH, CC and CO bonds [44], respectively, verifying their presence in the heterojunction. The above results demonstrated that GQDs and MoS2 were attached to the ZIS nanoflakes to construct the ternary heterojunction. The specific surface area and pore-size distribution were studied by the nitrogen adsorption–desorption experiments. All samples exhibited typical type-IV isotherms and type-H3 hysteresis loops (Fig. S8). The pore size distribution curves confirmed the mesoporous structure. The calculated specific surface areas of pure ZIS, ZIS/GQDs10, MoS210/ZIS and MoS210/ZIS/GQDs10 were 102.5 ± 0.9, 87.7 ± 1.5, 97.9 ± 0.7, and 99.9 ± 0.8 m2/g, respectively. The slight reduction in specific surface area after the addition of GQDs and MoS2 was consistent with previously reported CDs/ZIS and MoS2/ZIS composites [17,39], which is attributed to the obstruction of ZIS pores by GQDs or MoS2. Despite this, these variations did not significantly affect the enhanced photocatalytic activity of the heterojunctions, as detailed in Table S1 regarding pore size and volume.

The composition and chemical states of the samples were further elaborated by the XPS technique. The Zn, In, Mo, S and C elements were depicted in high-resolution spectra of the ternary MoS2/ZIS/GQDs heterojunction (Fig. 2 a–b). The binding energies at 445.07 eV and 452.57 eV in the In 3d spectrum corresponded to In 3d5/2 and In 3d3/2, respectively. Similarly, Zn 2p spectrum showed two peaks at 1021.89 eV and 1044.89 eV, which were attributed to Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2 of ZIS, respectively [5,25]. The S 2p spectrum of MoS2/ZIS/GQDs (Fig. 2b) could be resolved into two spin − orbit doublets, with the binding energies of 161.73 eV (161.85 eV) and 162.97 eV (163.08 eV) indicative of distinct chemical states of S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 for ZIS and MoS2 [8,43]. Meanwhile, the Mo 3d spectrum (Fig. 2b) exhibited three peaks at 231.55 eV, 228.41 eV and 226.11 eV, corresponding to Mo 4+ state and S 2s [45]. The presence of the Mo4+ was further confirmed by the Mo 3p spectrum, showing three distinct peaks at 412.43 eV, 400.42 eV and 394.83 eV for Mo 3p1/2, 3p3/2 and N 1s [46].

In addition, the binding energies of C 1s were located at 286.16 eV and 288.31 eV, corresponding to the CO and OCO groups within the GQDs, respectively [40,41]. The XPS analysis also revealed shifts in binding energies for various heterojunction elements compared to pure ZIS. Specifically, Zn 2p, In 3d, and S 3p peaks of MoS2/ZIS and ZIS/GQDs heterojunctions shifted towards higher binding energies (Fig. 2c–d, Fig. S9a). Conversely, the binding energies of Mo 3d, S 2p belonging to MoS2 component in MoS2/GQDs decreased in comparison to those of MoS2 (Fig. S9a–b), which corresponds to the variations observed in the C1s peaks of ZIS/GQDs and GQDs (Fig. S9c–d). The shifts in binding energy were ascribed to the differences in Fermi energy levels (E f) and surface charge density between MoS2 and ZIS (between ZIS and GQDs) [47]. A higher binding energy suggested a reduction in the charge density [48], indicating the electrons transfer from ZIS to MoS2 and GQDs at the heterojunction interfaces [25]. This electron transfer leads to the band bending and forming an IEF at their interfaces [22].

The photocatalytic H2 evolution was measured under the irradiation intensity of 100 mW/cm2 and a reaction temperature of ∼8 °C. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the H2 evolution rate for pure ZIS was 0.59 mmol h−1 g−1. When ZIS was combined with MoS2 or GQDs to form binary heterojunctions, the H2 generation activity significantly improved (Fig. 3a). Specifically, the H2 production rates for MoS2/ZIS and ZIS/GQDs peaked at optimal compositions in the MoS210/ZIS and ZIS/GQDs10 samples, achieving 15.17 mmol h−1 g−1 and 10.57 mmol h−1 g−1, respectively (Fig. S10a–b). The decrease in hydrogen evolution activity with further increases in GQDs or MoS2 can be attributed to the aggregation of zero-dimensional GQDs, which covers some active sites on ZIS [49], and the shading effect of excess 2D MoS2, which reduces light availability to the ZIS surface [16,34]. The MoS210/ZIS/GQDs10 heterojunction achieved an H2 production rate of 21.63 mmol h−1 g−1 and a total amount of 89.1 mmol/g within 4 h (Fig. S10c, Fig. 3a–b), which is about 37.3 times and 33.8 times higher than those of pure ZIS. These enhancements are attributed to the efficient separation and transfer of photoinduced carriers facilitated by the heterojunction structures [11,13,18–23,8,9]. Additionally, MoS2/ZIS/GQDs samples also demonstrated high photocatalytic stability, with no significant decline in H2 evolution observed after five consecutive cycles (Fig. 3c). XRD, SEM and XPS characterizations of the MoS210/ZIS/GQDs10 sample after the photocatalytic test revealed that the structural, morphological, and compositional characteristics of the MoS210/ZIS/GQDs10 sample remained consistent with pre-reaction conditions (Fig. S11) demonstrating the stability of this ternary catalyst.

Subsequently, the apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) of MoS210/ZIS/GQDs10 heterojunction was measured and calculated under different monochromatic light (400 nm, 420 nm, 500 nm and 600 nm, Supporting information, Table S2). Fig. 3d and Fig. S12 a–b illustrated the wavelength-dependent AQE of photocatalytic H2 evolution over MoS210/ZIS/GQDs10, MoS210/ZIS and ZIS/GQDs. As seen, the AQE values showed a strong correlation with the absorption spectra of the respective samples. The MoS210/ZIS/GQDs10 heterojunction exhibited the highest AQE values, achieving 17.60 ± 0.31 % at 400 nm and 15.19 ± 0.14 % at 420 nm. After comparison, the photocatalytic H2 production rate and AQE values of our ternary MoS2/ZIS/GQDs heterojunction were both superior to many other MoS2-ZIS or ZIS-carbon dots (CDs) catalysts (Fig. 3e) [34–36,39,42,50,51]. Besides, as shown in Table 1 , the performance was comparable to previously reported photocatalysts with different heterojunction types (type-Ⅰ, type-Ⅱ, Z-scheme and S-scheme) [13–15,24,26–31,47,52,53,7]. This outcome highlights the significant potential of the MoS210/ZIS/GQDs10 heterojunction in photocatalytic applications.

A series of characterizations were performed to investigate the inherent reasons for the enhanced photocatalytic H2 evolution of the ternary heterojunction. Fig. 4 a and Fig. S13 depicted the UV–Vis-NIR absorption spectra of ZIS, GQDs, MoS2, MoS210/ZIS, ZIS/GQDs10 and MoS210/ZIS/GQDs. It was found that ZIS primarily absorbs in the ultraviolet region, while the MoS2 component significantly boosts absorption in the visible light range of 500–750 nm. Moreover, the formation of binary and ternary heterojunctions significantly extended the absorption capacity into the visible and near-infrared regions, which promotes the generation of photo-carriers and enhances the photocatalytic performance. The photocurrent responses under simulated solar light source (100 mW/cm2) were shown in Fig. 4b. The photocurrent densities followed the order of MoS210/ZIS/GQDs10 > MoS210/ZIS>ZIS/GQDs10 > ZIS, consistent with the efficiency in photocatalytic H2 evolution. The high photocurrent density suggested more efficient separation and transfer of photoinduced carriers, highlighting the superiority of the MoS210/ZIS/GQDs10 heterojunction [8]. To verify these findings, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was carried out to analyze the charge transfer property [54]. As depicted in Fig. 4c, the arc radius of the MoS210/ZIS/GQDs10 in the low-frequency region was smaller than that of other samples, demonstrating the low interfacial resistance, which indicates faster electrochemical reaction kinetics resulting from the accelerated transfer of photoinduced charge carriers [55].

To gain a comprehensive understanding of the photocatalytic behavior, the band structures of MoS2, ZIS, and GQDs components were further calculated based on the absorption properties [12] (Fig. S13). The Tauc plots, revealed that the energy band gaps (E g) of MoS2, ZIS and GQDs were 1.61, 2.49 and 3.84 eV, respectively (Fig. 4d). E g value of MoS210/ZIS/GQDs10 heterojunction was calculated to be 2.40 eV (Fig. S14). Compared to the pure ZIS, the lower band gap facilitated the generation of the photoinduced electron-hole pairs. The flat-band potential ((E fb) values determined from Mott–Schottky (MS) curves was used to deduce conduction band (CB) positions [28,29]. MoS2, ZIS and GQDs with the positive curve slopes were identified as n-type semiconductors with E fb values of 0.02, −1.25 and −0.97 V (vs. NHE), respectively (Fig. 5 a–5c). Given that the conduction band edge (E CB) is ∼0.2 eV negative than the E fb values [5,56], E CB values of MoS2, ZIS and GQDs were calculated as −0.18, −1.45, and −1.17 eV (vs. NHE). Additionally, the valence band edge (E vB) values determined through the XPS valence spectra were about 1.43, 1.04 and 2.67 eV (vs NHE) (Fig. 5d-f). These values corroborate with EVB = E g + E CB. Fig. 6 a–c showed the UPS spectra of the MoS2, ZIS and GQDs, respectively. The cut-off energy (E cutoff) values (16.67 eV for MoS2, 17.43 eV for ZIS and 16.13 eV for GQDs) were determined by the intersections of the extrapolated straight lines. The work functions (Φ), representing the energy difference from the E f position to the vacuum level, were then calculated to be 4.55, 3.79 and 5.09 eV using the formula of Φ = 21.22 eV − E cutoff. Energy level diagrams of MoS2, ZIS and GQDs before and after contact were then plotted in Fig. 6d and Fig. S15. Notably, staggered band structures were observed between MoS2 (or GQDs) and ZIS, with the CB and VB levels of MoS2 and GQDs being lower than those of ZIS (Fig. 6d). The E f value of ZIS, obtained using the formula E f = E vacuum − Φ [23], was higher than the E f values of MoS2 and GQDs. Consequently, after ZIS contacts with MoS2 and GQDs, the electrons in ZIS would spontaneously transfer to MoS2 and GQDs until the E f equilibrium was achieved under the Fermi flattening effect (Fig. S15). This electron transfer resulted in the creation of IEF at the MoS2-ZIS and ZIS-GQDs interfaces. Simultaneously, the energy band edge of ZIS bent upwards, while that of MoS2 (or GQDs) bent downwards, forming the dual S‑scheme heterogeneous interfaces (Fig. 6e).

Based on the above characterizations and analysis, the mechanism accounting for the photocatalytic H2 evolution of ternary MoS2/ZIS/GQDs heterojunction was analyzed and illustrated in Fig. 6e. One of the keys to this improvement is the enhanced light absorption capacity of the MoS2/ZIS/GQDs heterojunction compared to pure ZIS, leading to the generation of more photoinduced carriers under simulated solar light irradiation. The photogenerated holes in the VB of MoS2 and GQDs were consumed for AA oxidation. The photoinduced electrons in the CB of MoS2 and GQDs were driven towards the VB of ZIS due to the IEF effect. This transfer leads to the recombination of these electrons with the photo-generated holes in the VB of ZIS to facilitate the spatial separation of electron-hole pairs. As a result, abundant photoinduced electrons with high reduction ability participate in the proton (H+) reduction reaction to produce a large amount of H2.

The electron spin resonance (ESR) was performed to detect superoxide (⋅O2 −) and hydroxyl (⋅OH) radicals to further verify the S-scheme charge transfer mechanism. As depicted in Fig. 6f, the MoS2, MoS2/ZIS and ZIS samples exhibited no DMPO-⋅O2 − response under dark conditions, and no DMPO-⋅OH response in the ZIS, GQDs and ZIS/GQDs samples. Upon 10 min of light irradiation, no obvious DMPO-⋅O2 − signal was detected in MoS2 and no significant DMPO-⋅OH signal was detected in ZIS (Fig. 6g-h), which is attributed to the mismatch between the conduction band edge (E CB) of MoS2 (−0.18 eV vs. NHE) and the valence band edge (E VB) of ZIS (1.04 eV vs. NHE) with the redox potentials necessary for the O2/·O2 (−0.33 eV vs. NHE at pH = 7) and H2O/·OH (1.94 eV vs. NHE at pH = 7) reactions, respectively [8,25]. Notably, the DMPO-⋅O2 − signal intensity of MoS2/ZIS was higher than that of ZIS, and ZIS/GQDs showed stronger DMPO-⋅OH signal than GQDs, indicating that more photoinduced electrons were resolved on the CB of ZIS in MoS2/ZIS sample, and more holes could be accumulated on the VB of GQDs in ZIS/GQDs heterojunction. These results reveal the S-scheme charge transfer mechanism in the MoS2/ZIS and ZIS/GQDs interfaces. The ESR spectra of ⋅OH for the MOS2/ZIS and⋅O2 − radicals for ZIS/GQDs were performed additionally. As shown in Fig. S16, MoS2/ZIS and GQDs/ZIS samples exhibit no DMPO-⋅O2 − and DMPO-⋅OH responses before irradiation. The high DMPO-⋅O2 − signal was detected after irradiation because the E CB of ZIS (−1.48 eV vs. NHE) and GQDs (−1.07 eV vs. NHE) are both higher than the redox potentials of O2/⋅O2 − (−0.33 eV vs. NHE at pH=7). In contrast, E VB of ZIS (1.04 eV vs. NHE) and MoS2 (1.43 eV vs. NHE) didn’t reach the redox potentials of H2O/⋅OH (1.94 eV vs. NHE at pH=7), suggesting no DMPO-⋅OH response. Therefore, it is worth mentioning that the ternary MoS2/ZIS/GQDs heterojunction, featuring dual S‑scheme interfaces, offers enhanced performance over the binary MoS2/ZIS or ZIS/GQDs, which possess a single S-scheme heterojunction. The dual S‑scheme configuration in the ternary system markedly accelerated charge separation, transfer, and the catalytic reaction, as corroborated by the observed results in photocatalytic H2 evolution experiments.

In summary, a dual S-scheme MoS2/ZIS/GQD heterojunction was successfully prepared by the facile hydrothermal method. Compared to various typical ZIS-based photocatalysts [7,13–15,24], this heterojunction exhibited high photocatalytic performance, reaching an H2 production rate of 21.65 mmol h−1 g−1 and the AQE of 17.60 % at 400 nm. The high photocatalytic performance of the heterojunction can be ascribed to several key factors: (i) Increased light absorption capacity, which significantly facilitated the generation of photoinduced carriers. (ii) The formation of dual S-scheme heterojunctions and two IEFs generated through electron transfer and rearrangement inhibited the recombination of photo-generated electron-hole pairs, thereby promoting efficient catalytic reactions in ZIS with abundant photoinduced electrons. The electron transfer, IEFs, the low charge transfer resistance and efficient separation and transfer of photoinduced carriers were proved through XPS, EIS and photocurrent response tests. This work provides a new perspective on the construction of multi-component heterostructures and highlights and anticipates their potential applications in sustainable energy technologies.

Ning Li: Writing – original draft. Jiafeng Ma: Writing – original draft. Wenlong Wang: Software, Resources, Data curation. Qing Chang: Resources, Data curation. Lei Liu: Resources, Data curation. Caihong Hao: Resources, Data curation. Huinian Zhang: Resources, Data curation. Huayang Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Shengliang Hu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology. Shaobin Wang: Resources, Methodology, Conceptualization.
